### Post by Verbeck on 2011-01-06
just got some new vga cables(3) but the monitor doesn't run at 1440*900 on any one of them. it isn't detected and shows 1042*768 as the highest possible. so how can i get the old resolution back? 

/proc/driver/nvidia/version
```

NVRM version: NVIDIA UNIX x86 Kernel Module  260.19.29  Wed Dec  8 12:09:09 PST 2010
GCC version:  gcc version 4.5.1 20101125 (prerelease) (GCC)

```

EndSection

```

edit: the cable that came with the monitor works fine

### Post by dcstar on 2011-01-07
> **Verbeck said:**
> works fine with another monitor

is there a way to force the resolution since its capable of running at 1440*900?

High resolutions require high-bandwidth cables, if the cables are not good enough then they cannot be used at the higher resolutions.

Look in the xorg.0.log file for the resolutions that are detected.

---

### Post by dandnsmith on 2011-01-07
You tell me - I've no idea, but one of my VGA cables has a pin missing in the middle row (pin 7 I think). As to whether I have all the others completely wired, I don't know that without laboriously testin with battery and a meter.

According to [this site](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VGA_connector) Pin1 is Red video, Pin14 is Vertical sync!

It's possible that Pin14 is involved in the acquisition of EDID info from the monitor, but I haven't done the necessary research.

---

### Post by tredegar on 2011-01-07
I looks like the new cables are not allowing the monitor's EDID information to be read properly (you could check by reading **xorg.log** after starting X with a new cable).

To solve this problem, you could save a copy of the monitor's EDID information when booting with the old cable. You'll find it at **/proc/acpi/video/NGFX/LCD/EDID**
Copy the EDID file to **/home/verbeck/EDID**

You already have an xorg.conf, good.

Now edit xorg.conf so it knows to get the EDID information from a file, not from probing the monitor. From looking at the xorg you posted, this should do it for you:
```
Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Device0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "Connected Monitor" "DFP-0"
    Option         "CustomEDID" "DFP-0: /home/verbeck/EDID"   
    SubSection     "Display"
        Modes      "1440x900" "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
EndSection
```

Swap to a new cable, restart X and it should be working at the correct resolution.

---

### Post by Verbeck on 2011-01-07
> **tredegar said:**
> I looks like the new cables are not allowing the monitor's EDID information to be read properly (you could check by reading **xorg.log** after starting X with a new cable).
:twisted:
```

[   909.292] (WW) NVIDIA(GPU-0): Unable to read EDID for display device CRT-1
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
[   909.296] (--) NVIDIA(0): Connected display device(s) on GeForce 8500 GT at PCI:1:0:0
[   909.296] (--) NVIDIA(0):     CRT-1
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
[   909.374] (WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to get display device CRT-1's EDID; cannot compute DPI
[   909.374] (WW) NVIDIA(0):     from CRT-1's EDID.

```
but there doesn't seem to be a video directory in /proc/acpi

### Post by tredegar on 2011-01-07
> [   909.374] (WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to get display device CRT-1's EDID; cannot compute DPI
It cant get the EDID. 
> but there doesn't seem to be a video directory in /proc/acpi
There won't be, because it can't get the EDID.

Was this with the old cable? Using the old cable gives you the correct resolution, so presumably it is reading the EDID correctly, and the NVIDIA driver (which you are running) puts the EDID in /proc/... for you. The new cables don't allow the EDID to be read (poor quality cables IMO).

Otherwise, with the _old cable_ if you fire up System -Admin - **nvidia X server settings** you'll see your monitor listed in the column on the left. Click that, and you'll be given the option to Acquire EDID. Save it to a file, then as my post at #9.

---

### Post by Verbeck on 2011-01-07
it gives the same error as before in the log file and also displays the **'**No Signal' message, so i had to restore the old xorg.conf


and since they are [s]probably[/s] surely low quality cables, lets call it solved. 
thanx for help tredegar, Dereck, David  and pricetech :)
